items.q: Are abstraction and decomposition the same thing?
items.correct: No - decomposition splits a problem into smaller parts, while abstraction removes irrelevant detail from the problem; they are separate, complementary techniques.
items.misconception: Yes - both just mean simplifying a problem, so they are identical.
items.random_wrong: Yes - abstraction is what decomposition is called in flowcharts.
items.why: Both 'make problems easier', so they collapse together. Test yourself: splitting (decomposition) versus hiding detail (abstraction) - exam questions ask you to tell them apart.

items.q: Must pseudocode follow the exact syntax of a real programming language?
items.correct: No - pseudocode is structured English for humans: it must be clear and unambiguous, but no compiler will ever check its syntax.
items.misconception: Yes - pseudocode with a missing semicolon is wrong.
items.random_wrong: Yes - pseudocode must be valid Python.
items.why: Students who learn one language treat its syntax as law. Pseudocode is judged on logic and clarity, freeing you to plan before worrying about syntax.

items.q: In a flowchart, is a rectangle used for a decision?
items.correct: No - a rectangle shows a process or action; decisions use a diamond, which has separate exits for yes and no.
items.misconception: Yes - rectangles show decisions and diamonds show calculations.
items.random_wrong: Yes - any shape can be used for any step.
items.why: Shape conventions feel arbitrary so they get scrambled. The diamond's two labelled exits are the giveaway: only decisions branch.

items.q: Breaking a large problem into smaller, more manageable sub-problems is called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decomposition. “Iteration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The purpose of a trace table is to:
items.correct: Record variable values step by step to follow or debug an algorithm
items.why: It's Record variable values step by step to follow or debug an algorithm. “Translate code into machine code” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a flowchart, a diamond symbol represents:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A decision. “An input” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The London Underground map shows stations and connections but ignores true distances. This is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Abstraction. “Overflow” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In an algorithm, x starts at 2 and is doubled on each of 3 loop iterations. What is the final value of x?
items.why: The real figure is 16. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
